data.title: U-Haul: Moving Forward (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

U-Haul Holding Company (NYSE:UHAL) is the largest “do-it-yourself” moving and storage operator in North America. They have three main businesses (U-Move, Self-Storage, and U-Box) to serve customers' moving needs.

U-Haul has the largest rental vehicle fleet in North America, and they are #3 in the self-storage business and #2 player in portable moving and storage businesses.

Post-pandemic, U-Haul enjoyed large revenue growth in 2022 and 2023. After experiencing a contraction in 2024, U-Haul resumed growth that is more in line with their historical trajectory.

I believe U-Haul is a great option for an investor seeking a long-term, steady-growth company.

  • U-Haul's growth trajectory has normalized, and their revenue has resumed a growth trajectory in line with their historical pattern.
  • U-Haul has great operating cash-generating ability and a solid balance sheet to support their growth.
  • The need for self-storage and moving business will keep increasing in the future.

Normalizing Growth Trend

U-Haul was growing steadily before the pandemic. Between 2015 and 2020, U-Haul's revenue grew 5.3% per year. After going through a very unusual growth/shrink between 2021 and 2023, the self-moving market stabilized, allowing growth to normalize.

Revenue for 4Q 2024 was $1.3 B, which was 3.6% higher than 4Q 2023. The self-moving equipment rental revenue increased by 4.6% YOY in 4Q 2024. This 3.4% and 4.6% YOY growth is well within the historical growth trajectory of U-Haul, and I expect this trend to continue in the future.

As revenue growth has returned to normal, the profit margins (net income margin, gross profit margin, and EBITDA margin) are going back to their historical ranges as well. For example, the net income margins of U-Haul had been around 9-12% between 2015 and 2020. The net income margin went up to nearly 20% during the initial post-pandemic years, but it dropped to the 7% range in 2023.

The profit margin has shown an improvement in the past couple of quarters, and I expect the margins to continue their way back to the historical range.

Operating Cash Flow and Balance Sheet

U-Haul has been generating strong cash flows, which has created a strong balance sheet. In the past 5 years, U-Haul generated well over $1 B per year of operating cash flow ($1.1 B, $1.5 B, $1.9 B, $1.7 B, and $1.5 B from 2020 to 2024).

Thanks to this strong operating cash-generating ability, U-Haul has been maintaining a strong balance sheet. They have total cash of $1.2 B ($5.19 per share), and their current ratio and covered ratio are 1.97x and 2.60x, respectively, representing strong liquidity and a solid ability to service the debt.

The strong cash flow and balance sheet have allowed U-Haul to make strategic investments to support their growth. In the past twelve months, U-Haul invested $3.5 B in capital expenditure, including $1.8 B in their rental fleet and $1.5 B in their real estate footprint expansion.

Over the past 10 years, U-Haul consistently reinvested their cash flow in fleet growth ($2.2 B), fleet rotation ($3.4 B), and real estate investment ($7.9 B).

Given this consistent reinvestment, I expect U-Haul to continue their growth well into the future, and to remain a market leader in the self-moving and self-storage business for the foreseeable future.

Need for Self-Storage and Moving Business

The self-storage market size is expected to grow at 5.9% CAGR until 2030. The main drivers for the growth are urbanization, population growth, and expansion of e-commerce.

Also, there are still a significant number of metro areas in the U.S. that are underserved. These markets are often characterized by low per capita inventory and rising demand, and the characteristics offer opportunities for self-storage companies to expand their markets. New England, Western, and Mid-Western metro areas can benefit most from the expansion of self-storage at this point.

Self-moving service is also expected to grow along with the self-storage business. Globally, the self-storage and moving services market is forecasted to grow at 9.3% CAGR between 2025 and 2029. Growing real-estate investment and increasing demand for flexible storage solutions and seamless moving experiences are the main drivers for this growth.

U-Haul is in a prime position to take advantage of these macro tailwinds. Including the independent dealer network, there are more than 24,000 locations that serve customers, and 90% of the U.S. population lives within 5 miles of a U-Haul dealer.

With this vast network of dealers and self-storage locations, U-Haul should capture a substantial portion of the industry growth in the U.S.

Risk

For obvious reasons, there is a strong correlation between the home sale activity level and the moving activity level. Due to the high home prices and mortgage rates in the past couple of years, home sale activity has been trending down.

For example, pending home sales in January 2025 dropped 4.6% YOY, and with the elevated interest rate and inflation risk, the home sale activity may stay low for a while. This reduced activity in the real estate market will negatively impact U-Haul's business.

U-Haul's Return on Invested Capital has been trending downward for the past couple of years. While I believe this is a temporary setback due to high material and real estate acquisition costs, this downward trend may indicate a fewer number of profitable growth opportunities for U-Haul. Therefore, investors should monitor if U-Haul's new growth initiatives pan out.

Conclusion

U-Haul has been a steady growth stock with a market-leading position for a long time. Then the pandemic majorly rocked their growth trajectory. A crazy amount of migration during the pandemic increased their revenue dramatically in 2022 and 2023. Normalization of the moving activities in 2024 caused a sharp decrease in profit from this peak.

Going forward, I expect U-Haul to resume their pre-pandemic growth rate, which was solid and steady.

Macroeconomic tailwinds from the increasing demand for self-storage and moving services will benefit U-Haul, while their strategic investment will enable them to capture the lion's share of the macro-tailwinds.

The slowdown in the real estate market and fewer investment opportunities may challenge U-Haul, but I expect them to handle these.

Investors who are looking for a steady growth company should check out U-Haul.

data.title: German Inflation Remains Unchanged In February
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

The just-released flash estimate of German inflation for February brought some small relief for the ECB, showing headline inflation unchanged at 2.3% year-on-year. Core inflation came down to 2.6% YoY from 2.9% YoY. The European inflation measure came in unchanged at 2.8% YoY.

Earlier today, retail sales and labour market data indicated that expectations for a consumption-driven economic recovery are likely to be dashed once again. While the meagre increase in retail sales by 0.2% month-on-month in January was too little to offset the weak final quarter of 2024, the very gradual turning of the labour market continued in February.

Headline inflation to remain between 2% and 2.5% this year

The available regional state data suggests that favourable energy base effects as well as falling prices for alcohol, healthcare and household goods put downward pressure on inflation, while food prices accelerated again. Services inflation also continued its very gradual easing trend.

Looking ahead, one important driver of headline inflation will still be energy prices, which have gone on a rollercoaster ride in recent months. A ride that could easily continue, depending on whether geopolitical tensions soften or escalate. Regarding underlying inflation, however, there will be two opposing trends determining the future path of inflation. On the one hand, the cooling of the labour market should take away wage pressures and consequently also inflationary pressure, while on the other hand, the delayed pass-through of higher services costs is still in full swing. At the same time, the recent increase in selling-price expectations in industry is also concerning and could be aggravated by upcoming European tariffs.

As a result of these opposing trends, we see German headline inflation settling down in the range of 2% to 2.5% throughout the year, or to paraphrase the ECB: “close to but above 2%”.

Today's inflation data from eurozone countries paves the way for an ECB rate cut next week

For the ECB, today’s inflation data, not only from Germany but also from France and Italy, will have cemented the case for another 25-basis point rate cut next week. The main question, however, will be what’s next for the ECB. At 2.5%, the policy rate would be at the upper end of the neutral interest rate range. More hawkish ECB officials, like Isabel Schnabel, have started to push back against additional rate cuts. The critical communication to watch next week is whether the ECB drops the “restrictive” label from its official stance. Given the currently unprecedented high level of uncertainty, a complete drop of the “restrictive” label might be too hawkish. Some modification, e.g. towards “less restrictive” or “hardly restrictive anymore” might be better options to signal a possible end to the current autopilot.

In any case, given the structural weakness of the eurozone economy as well as looming tariffs and lower inflationary pressure on the back of a turn in the labour market, we still think that eventually the ECB will have to bring rates down to at least 2% to make sure that rates are no longer restrictive but possible even accomodative.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: AAON: Don't Get Sucked In After A 22% Dip
data.description:
Introduction

AAON Inc (NASDAQ:AAON) has been a name I’ve followed for a while and in March 2024, I explained why shares of AAON were priced for perfection and that the company’s valuation was in need of cooling down. Since then, this seems to have been the right call as shares marched higher up until yesterday when shares fell 23% on the back of poor earnings. Overall, I’d characterize 2024 as a year of challenges, but even with gross margins expected to return to 2023 levels in the next two years and headwinds to dissipate by Q2 2025, I think shares are richly priced, and it seems that the market might be underestimating some of the risks here.

Unimpressive Top and Bottom Line Results

AAON is an expensive stock that’s seen pretty remarkable growth over the last decade. In the last ten years, the company has grown both revenues and EBITDA at mid-teens CAGRs driven by both organic and inorganic growth. The company’s FY 2024, however, presented somewhat of a different picture as sales fell to $297.7 million, a decrease of 2.9% and a miss of $12.1 million relative to consensus estimates.

Before analyzing the income statement, I think it’s important to note that after a significant boost to revenues in 2022, growth has started to moderate. As for what led to the decline in Q4 2024, most of the decline was largely a result of the company’s Oklahoma segment.

In the company’s Oklahoma segment, sales fell by 16.1% because of a refrigerant transition where the company stopped accepting orders for using the legacy 410A refrigerant in September 2024 and Q4 2024 was the first quarter in which they only accepted orders for equipment configured with the new 454B refrigerant. Bookings of rooftop units in the fall season were unimpressive because many state building codes weren’t updated to allow 454B equipment until Q4 (leading the company to bring down production more than expected, resulting in lower volumes).

The impact of this was pretty severe on margins. For example, overall company gross profit margins contracted significantly to 26.1% compared to 36.4% in the year prior. This compression in profitability was primarily driven by the lower sales volumes within the AAON Oklahoma segment, leading to a deleveraging of fixed costs. Additionally, temporary margin declines were observed in the other segments like AAON Coil Products and BASX as the company invested in expanding capacity for longer term growth. As such, even with a marginal increase of 0.7% in SG&A (16.2% as a percentage of sales), EPS of $0.30 came in well below the $0.56 last year and missed consensus estimates by 18 cents.

Despite the challenges faced in the income statement, AAON's balance sheet remained relatively strong. At year-end, the company had $154.9 million of debt against a cash position of $6.5 million. While cash is de minimis, the company has sufficient liquidity with $313.3 million of working capital. Leverage also isn’t a concern, as the company has a net leverage ratio of 0.57x.

Outlook

In terms of the outlook, I don’t expect sales to plummet by any means, but I do expect some softness going forward. On the bright side, backlog and bookings increased 70% and 62%, respectively, highlighting that the trough that the company had been experiencing might be behind them. So far, it seems like growth from data center bookings (that require cooling systems and HVAC technology) has been a source of growth, and I expect that to continue. With backlog at $867.1 million now, there’s good visibility into revenue growth going forward, however, given that backlog is pretty lumpy over time, I have less confidence in the longer term outlook.

On the company’s conference call post-results, when asked about the potential for the data center business to become a larger percentage of overall sales, management indicated that while it's difficult to project with certainty they believe the data center market has the potential to be a "significant growth driver" for the company going forward. Another interesting point was that AAON is exploring opportunities to expand its product offerings into new markets and applications. When asked about M&A opportunities, management was very forthcoming in discussing their desire to find potential acquisition targets, which tells me that the company is focused on in organic growth as well. With a leverage ratio of less than one turn, they certainly have the balance sheet to make it work.

In the near term, management anticipates that the first quarter will mirror the fourth quarter of 2024 with sequential improvements expected throughout the year. The AAON Coil Products segment is projected to experience ‘solid sequential improvement in sales and profits’ starting in Q2 as the company works through engineering and design modifications on the BASX branded data center liquid cooling product. As for what to expect for 2025, management guided for revenues to grow in the ‘mid- to high-teens’ with gross margins at similar levels to what they saw in 2024 and SG&A as a percentage of sales to decline by 25bps to 50bps. So outside of top line growth to drive earnings growth, I don’t expect EBITDA and EPS to grow by much this year. To the extent that the refrigerant transition is a temporary issue, I do think that the decline in the share price following the quarter is warranted though.

Risks and Valuation

AAON trades expensively any way we look at it. On P/E, the company trades for 27.1x forward earnings, which is well above the peer group average at 22.9x (Source: Bloomberg). But I think there are several reasons beyond just relative valuation about why investors ought to stay away.

For one thing, capex is a major line item that doesn’t get deducted from EBITDA. As an example, AAON is currently building out their new Memphis facility, which won’t be cheap. While the facility will be ready for production sometime this year, I don’t think the incremental returns from revenue growth from the guide suggest strong internal IRRs on this organic investment. With $200 million of total projected capex of the year, AAON is spending a large portion of the $272.2 million of EBITDA that they generated in FY 2024.

Last year, capex came in above the $100 million mark, and I expect a good portion of this is also maintenance capex. As capex has averaged around $75 million per year in the last five years, the ‘adjusted’ EV/EBITDA multiple (subtracting capex from EBITDA) would be close to a 40x multiple on the stock. This implies that shares are likely overvalued at current levels. Following the results today, I’d expect a number of downgrades to come, as most analysts haven’t yet upgraded their price targets.

As for the other risks, I think there’s a possibility that the headwinds related to the refrigerant transition could be longer than expected. While management believes this is temporary, there is a risk that the transition could be more protracted than anticipated, potentially impacting sales and profitability. The Memphis facility should also help greatly as we will be moving some high-volume production from Redmond to this facility later this year, which will result in higher production efficiency in Redmond, but I’m not confident given management has missed guidance this quarter.

Another risk is that the company’s ambitious growth plans, particularly in the data center market, are subject to execution risk. The BASX segment is experiencing growing pains due to capacity constraints and operational inefficiencies and furthermore, the initial ramp-up of production for the new BASX branded data center liquid cooling product has been delayed. There is a risk in my view that these issues could persist or that the company may face challenges in scaling up production to meet the growing demand.

Conclusion

Overall, I remain cautious about both the near-term and long-term outlook for AAON. The company’s valuation is still on the high side and doesn’t seem to fully account for the risks associated with the refrigerant transition and the operational challenges within its BASX segment. While there are positives like increasing backlog and bookings, and opportunities in new markets, the risks tied to execution, production delays, and especially capex are concerning to me. Given the stock’s elevated price and the uncertainties surrounding its growth plans, I believe investors should avoid the stock for now.

data.title: Shoals Technologies Q4: Why I'm Sticking With My Buy Rating
data.description:
Thesis

Shoals Technologies Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:SHLS) is America’s leading producer of electric balance of system (EBOS) installations for the solar market. Think: the complex wiring and electrical components that are necessary for transferring the electric current from the solar panels themselves to the inverter and eventually to the power grid. The company released its 4th quarter end-year results earlier this week. Shares fell below a critical support level in response. Given the long-term positive outlook for solar power, as it remains an integral piece of what will likely become a mounting global energy crisis following rapidly escalating AI power demands, I perceive this as an overreaction. The shares, in general, are very cheap. My conviction is based on the slow and steady growth of solar energy as an increasingly critical component of energy and the likely shift in sentiment that this will bring. I continue to rate them a buy.

I previously covered Shoals about 5 weeks ago, whereupon I provided a fairly detailed analysis of the company’s background, market share, litigation issues, industry head/tailwinds, and recent price action. Shares were hovering just above a base of support that had formed around $4.15. Earnings results have since sent shares below this level, and they now sit at an all-time low, down 76% in the last twelve months, and 91% from all-time highs. While I don’t expect Shoals to reach those heights anytime soon, I am expecting a slow and steady recovery in the share price as the broader macro environment for solar energy improves. In this article, I will analyze the latest results and offer commentary based on the company’s guidance and comments from management.

Background

Based in Portland, TN and founded in 1996, Shoals has become the market leader in the US for EBOS installations, which are crucial, mission-critical features of solar installations.

Their patent-protected Big-Lead Assembly (BLA) system was, as of 2020, installed in 32% of US solar installations. While the company has yet to provide an update on its market share, it is nevertheless instructive to hear that management is targeting a 60% market share going forward.

Their steadily increasing dominance in this field has led to several key partnerships, with the leading US-based solar panel manufacturer First Solar, Inc. (FSLR) and the leading US-based solar tracking company Nextracker Inc. (NXT). Their main competitors in the EBOS market – notably, Voltage and Hikam America – use imported products, as far as I am aware, and also do much of their manufacturing outside the US. This bodes well for Shoals, which does its manufacturing in the US – their new factory will be in Tennessee, a “red” state – in the context of incoming and potential tariffs on overseas production administered by the current Trump administration. On this issue, CEO Brandon Moss had this to say in the Q&A portion of the latest earnings call:

"Look, just maybe as a reminder to everybody, we're a domestic manufacturer and we leverage a mostly domestic supply chain. So at a customer's request, we will import products from time to time to incorporate in our products. And we're working with suppliers and customers to mitigate any tariff exposure that we've got. But we've got, by and large, domestic products and domestic alternates for products for our bill of materials. So we feel like we'll be able to weather a tariff storm probably better than anybody."

As for solar power in general, the market globally is growing and all estimates suggest the global solar market will continue to expand at an impressive clip. It could grow at approximately a 20% CAGR over the next few years, as I highlighted in my previous report.

Approximately 60% of installations are “utility-scale” meaning spread out in large rows covering vast acres of open space, rather than on rooftops or elsewhere. The former is Shoals’ target market. According to a recent report by the International Energy Agency, 12.6% of global power is estimated to come from solar in 2028, up from just 2.1% in 2018.

Solar trackers are now included in approximately 94% of all new utility-scale installations, and within these, the price of steel represents approximately two-thirds of the total cost. This is significant, and it follows that the rising or falling price of steel would lead to the decreasing or increasing likelihood of these projects being undertaken. Therefore, it is encouraging to see that the price of steel has actually decreased slightly from my last analysis, now sitting at around 3275 CNY/T, from over 3300 several weeks ago. This indicates some price stability, which is important for the industry and should help restore or shore up confidence in project development and completion rates going forward. Conversely, marked price fluctuations could cause those financing projects to either pull back and withdraw or wait it out and push back project start times in the hopes of securing a better deal. Both are bad news for the solar industry, so price stability or at the very least only slowing increasing or decreasing steel prices bodes well.

Labour availability remains a problem – as Shoals’ management has highlighted in previous calls – but the company is advantaged in this environment given its BLA system does not require certified electricians to install, but rather general laborers. More generally, solar projects remain the most cost-effective of all energy solutions, as solar has the “lowest levelized cost of energy (LCOE) and the fastest deployment time,” as noted by management during the Q4 2024 earnings call.

That the LCOE is set to continue declining – and quite rapidly, regardless of whether considering conservative (blue), moderate (orange) or advanced (green) projections – is positive for Shoals. All of this is to say that, eventually, so long as they can maintain if not increase their market share in EBOS installations – again, a crucial component of utility-scale solar projects – then Shoals will surely be a beneficiary. Patient investors will be rewarded. However, what about the company’s most recent results, and is the turnaround/rebound in both business prospects and sentiment for the solar industry in general likely to come anytime soon?

Latest Earnings and Projections

As highlighted in their latest earnings call, Shoals earned $107m in revenue for the 4 quarter of 2024, booked $145m in new projects and determined their backlog to be in the region of $635m. Of this, they expect $440m to be earmarked for 2025, with the rest in 2026 and beyond. With their full year 2024 revenue at $399m, this would represent just over a 10% growth rate into 2025. Adding in some volatility and potential project delays, the company officially guided for $410-450m. If they can reach the high end of this, it would represent an even better 12.8% growth rate in revenue from 2024-25.

As for earnings, the company uses adjusted EBITDA as its preferred metric. In Q4, they earned $26.4m (with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 25%), and of this, $14m was converted into operating cash flow. With just $1.5 cap-ex spend, this represented $12.5m in FCF; an impressive 11.9% FCF yield.

These figures represented a drop from 2023, which management attributed to the challenging business environment in 2024. Going forward, management is expecting $100-115 in adjusted EBITDA for the full year 2025, but this will cut into free cash flow due to higher cap-ex spending due to the construction of their new factory. Despite the $25-35m cap-ex spend for full year 2025, they still anticipate being cash flow positive, which is impressive to say the least, and to my mind speaks to the company’s strong execution in this challenging environment.

In Q4 2024, their gross margin declined slightly to 37.6%, which management attributed to political uncertainty (I assume the impact of tariffs), labor and equipment availability, and regulatory and permitting delays. Project delays – the main consequence – were not limited to Shoals or the solar industry, as Wood Mackenzie noted that 53 gigawatts of pipeline projects experienced a delay during the year. Looking forward, Shoals anticipates recovering to a 40% gross margin due to an improving business environment more generally, which seems very reasonable given they have achieved this previously.

The company used the challenging 2024 to invest in innovative solutions and products. These included new designs on its BLA systems and combiners/recombiners, aimed to lower costs and improve efficiencies for their customers.

CEO Moss remarked on the current environment:

"As we've seen over the past 2 months, the federal regulatory dynamic is prone to rapid changes in course which requires us to be flexible in our planning and approach. However, we believe the outlook for U.S. utility-scale solar is improving. This is supported by both the conversations with our customers and the strength we've seen in our 2025 book of business."

I share his confidence in the sector’s recovery, and I was pleased to hear that during the year, management doubled down on its focus on satisfying customer needs. They were able to diversify their customer mix and, alongside product innovations, they also worked to expand internationally – winning $8m in new project revenue from Chile and Australia, with a projected $86m pipeline going forward – and streamlined their sales practices. They also worked towards consolidating manufacturing into a single site and improving efficiencies through automation at their new factory in Tennessee. I see this as a very worthwhile investment that will pay off in the future, with higher gross margins.

While management has not disclosed the economics of this new facility and what it hopes are the cost savings, I trust they would not embark on this project without ensuring that it improves their business prospects and margins. Indeed, they are in a privileged position to be able to afford to invest in a new state-of-the-art facility. This has come as a result of their growth over the years, FCF generation and careful deployment of cash from operations.

Speaking of which, Shoals retains a strong balance sheet. As reported in Q4 2024, they have $235m in cash and equivalents, and it is good to see that inventory has declined over recent years and quarters, which shows that their products are in demand. Their assets-to-liabilities ratio has improved over recent quarters, and shareholders equity has now reached its highest figure, as debt has been paid down to just $142.87 from $239.75m in Q1 2023. Their current ratio is a healthy 2.33, and their debt-to-equity ratio is 0.26.

Litigation Uncertainties

The company is currently involved in two ongoing litigation uncertainties. The first is related to faulty wiring that customers have complained about and demanded recompense. Management has provided updates on this issue over the last few earnings calls, and it is positive to see that they have reported a declining figure in both G&A expenses (I’d assume mostly litigation costs) and future remediation cost liability, now down to $28.4 million. Shoals is currently suing Prysmian, the company that supplied the faulty wiring that Shoals supplied and installed for its customers. Their legal case against them is heading toward the discovery and deposition phase, which should be completed by mid-2025, according to management. I see no reason why Shoals should not win this case, but that is not a matter for me to speculate in any case.

The second litigation uncertainty surrounds a claim of patent infringement against their competitor, Voltage. After a see-saw battle in the courts that ended in what Shoals management perceived to be an unfair ruling, the company has added two further patents to the case (#375 and #376), which they believe provides ‘additional protection for our BLA product line’. They also appealed an earlier decision by the ITC to not uphold the judge’s initial determination (in Shoals’s favor). Both of these issues have created some uncertainty, and investors should consider the potential outcomes – one way or the other – in their determinations of value for the company.

Company Valuation and Concluding Thoughts

Shoals currently trades with a forward P/E of 9.11 and a P/S of 1.64; from these measures, the company has never been cheaper. Shoals is guiding for, at the mid-range, $107.5m in adjusted EBITDA, up from $99.1m in 2024, thereby a growth rate of 8.5% and a PEG ratio of 1.07. If they reach the high end of their guidance ($115m), the PEG drops to 0.57 which represents a tantalizing bargain at current prices.

These valuations also do not consider share buybacks, as management has $125m left in its share repurchase authorization, which, if spent entirely in 2025, would reduce the market cap by a whopping 21.5%. I don’t necessarily expect them to do this, though, especially as they will need some of their surplus cash for their capital expenditures in relation to constructing their new factory. However, the depressed share price right now would give management an excellent opportunity to reward shareholders in this way. Any sign of buybacks will be received positively by shareholders and could move the needle in terms of its share price.

What we need to consider is whether this is a value trap. I’d suggest not. Given they can still generate FCF even in a challenging environment, any signs of improving fundamentals and the share price could reverse rapidly. Once revenue and earnings show growth, so will the share price and, likely also, the multiple that investors will be willing to pay for the company.

Analysts seem to agree. Of those who updated their share price projections this week (13 of the 15 covering the stock), 9 have reiterated their buy recommendations, with 4 recommending to hold. None of them downgraded the stock. The average price target across all analysts is currently $6.61, representing 70% upside from the closing price on Thursday afternoon.

It is clear that Shoals has taken a beating, much like the rest of the solar energy industry as an outcome of a shift in sentiment over the past year or so. However, this is a profitable company at the heart of the sector, with a dominant market share and supported by various industry tailwinds. I have no doubt the company will recover, but how long the challenging environment for solar projects will last is uncertain. With the latest decline in Shoals’ share price, I do feel that much of the negativity is now priced in.

This is proving to be a very cyclical industry, and investors should be prepared for the volatility that this inevitably brings. I rated them a buy when they were trading for a dollar more, and I have no reason to downgrade at this point. In fact, the only thing stopping me from upgrading to a strong buy is the uncertainty regarding their litigation outcomes. As a result, I’m sticking with my buy recommendation.

data.title: Shelton Fixed Income Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market Commentary

We waited so long for this?

The Fed finally began the rate cut cycle in September with a 50-basis point double shot, followed by further 25 bp cuts in November and December. At the same time, longer-term interest rates shot higher as concerns around a recurrence of inflation rose to the surface. The subsequent outcome of the election triggered additional worries about stronger growth, tariffs, and higher deficits, causing the curve to steepen even more. The updated Summary of Economic Projections at the December FOMC meeting illustrated that the Fed was shifting back away from labor market concern and back to a more balanced assessment of the risks of their dual mandate. As we will discuss in the outlook section, where we go from here could almost be anywhere, but at least we'll be starting from attractive all-in yields.

GDP moved slightly higher from 3.0% in Q2 to 3.1% in Q3. As of January 1st, Q4 is currently tracking to a 2.54% figure according to the Atlanta Fed GDPNow Forecast, after this estimate had been as high as 3.36% earlier in December. The cumulative effect of higher rates has yet to dent economic activity, and the series of rate cuts and expectations of further cuts that prevailed from August through October increased the enthusiasm for growth. The key question now is whether this significant move higher in longer-term rates starts to bite and have an effect on business and consumer spending.

At year-end, markets are pricing in less than two more cuts in 2025 and another one cut in 2026. The long end of the curve will also be impacted by philosophical issues like R* and the terminal rate, and by expectations around fiscal policy, deficit spending and budgetary restraint (or lack thereof). As 2025 progresses, we will get more clarity on fiscal policy, tariffs, immigration reform and other factors which will all bear upon inflation, employment, and growth.

Although the balance of risks has likely shifted back into an equilibrium between inflation and full employment after recent employment reports, the FOMC still has a very difficult task of ensuring inflation does not reappear or reaccelerate, while also protecting the labor market from further deterioration. Fed governors have spoken about how they do not want any further weakening of the labor markets from here. These things operate with a lag, so if all the cumulative tightening over the last several years is still impacting hiring and firing decisions, we won't know until later that the employment picture was still deteriorating. In addition, we now have the added uncertainties of geopolitical volatility under the new administration.

In Q4 2024, the Fund posted returns of -1.48% (DEBIX) and -1.53% (DEBTX). Below is a table of returns for the Fund, various relevant indices, and the Morningstar Non-traditional Bond category. While performance in the quarter was negative, relative performance versus indices, especially adjusted for the average creditworthiness of the portfolio (BBB-/BB+), was favorable. Full year performance significantly exceeds the Agg and the Investment Grade corporate and municipal bond indices, but slightly trailed the Nontraditional bond category. The Fund did trail the relevant high yield index, which was powered by very strong performance from the CCC cohort. Our crossover-rated positioning has been purposeful and diligent, which has allowed us to navigate the uncertainties of the rate cut cycle. We attribute this to our credit selection and ability to find unique, uncorrelated investments, our interest rate hedges, and our dynamic and tactical adjustments to the portfolio as macroeconomic and single-name data evolves. Our longer-term performance remains very strong and is a testament to our ability to overcome the vagaries of the rates markets and the occasional idiosyncratic hiccups by stringing together significantly more idiosyncratic winners than losers over time. In December 2023, the fund reached its 10-year anniversary, and the performance of the Fund since inception stands very tall relative to peers, relevant indices, and the Non-traditional bond category.

Portfolio weightings were higher in corporate bonds and lower in municipal bonds. We also did not have any cash credit short positions in acknowledgement of the significantly higher cost of carry and the need to carry positions longer given expectations for the delayed onset of a recession. Although we appreciate the all-in high yields for corporate bonds, spreads are tight and uncertainties abound, so we maintained our portfolio hedge on the IG CDX index during the quarter. When interest rates declined on expectations for significant additional rate cuts, and rate volatility was subdued, we added back rate hedges, which served us well late in the quarter.

Corporate bond long positions were a drag on performance overall during the quarter. At the ratings level, our single B and CCC positions were strong positive contributors, and our higher quality longer duration bonds suffered as interest rates increased sharply and the curve steepened. Favorable credit selection allowed us to avoid any major downside surprises. We continued to strike a good balance between prudent credit selection in single B and select CCC credits we believe are underappreciated by the market and rating agencies, while avoiding weaker single Bs and CCCs which could be severely punished in a downturn.

The borrowers in our portfolio reported solid Q3 earnings generally across the board and offered favorable yet prudent Q4 and initial 2025 guidance as well, reinforcing our decision to upgrade the quality of the portfolio in the face of cost pressures and a potential Fed-induced economic slowdown. We enjoyed some single-name outperformance where better than expected earnings were applauded, credit improvement was recognized by the markets and rating agencies, or corporate transactions were announced. We expect this dispersion, where strong performance is rewarded, and poor performance is penalized, will accelerate going forward. Issuers that materially miss earnings, call off corporate transactions, or otherwise disappoint their investors will be severely punished. This kind of market is highly conducive to our strategy, when markets are generally rangebound or sideways and individual credit selection is rewarded or punished.

The Fund benefitted from idiosyncratic gains in i) BlueTriton Brands, which completed its merger with Primo Brands; ii) AMC Entertainment and Cinemark, which rose on strong film box office results; and iii) GEO Group, which rose sharply on expectations for increased revenue under the incoming Trump administration.

The top 5 contributors and detractors for the quarter (9/30/24 - 12/31/24) are listed below:

Corporate Commentary

The lower the quality, the better the return proved to be the overall theme in corporate credit. High yield spreads started the year at +333bp, peaked at +381bp in August, reached the tights of +253bp in November, before finishing the year at +287bp. Higher quality, rate sensitive bonds were punished by the sharp move higher in rates, while lower quality bonds clipped their higher coupons and were unimpeded by credit risk, as spreads tightened in the risk-on environment. How long CCCs can continue to outperform is the key question.

In investment grade, duration was punished throughout the year, with long bonds returning -1.95% vs. intermediate maturity bonds achieving +4.22% annual gains. Ironically and correlated with longer duration, highest quality bonds suffered the most in Q4 '24 with AAA's returning -5.88%, AA's -3.94%, single A's -3.31%, and BBB's -2.58%. Investment grade funds had an overall quarterly inflow of $35.7 billion, and high yield funds had an inflow of $3.3 billion. New issuance for investment grade was a massive $258 billion, beating out the $228 billion this quarter last year. High yield new issuance was also very strong with companies bringing $65 billion in new supply as compared to $42 billion in 4Q23. The market was open to all issuers, with CCC's bringing ~$4 billion in new issuance, versus ~$1 billion this quarter last year. Refinancing or repayment of debt continued to be the most common use of proceeds, accounting for 80% of YTD issuance, the highest since 2000 and a trend we expect to continue as issuers peck away at the much publicized "wall of maturities." For the year, the sectors that brought the most in issuance were Financials with $62 billion, followed by Consumer with $59 billion.[1,2]

The balance sheets of HY issuers continue to appear in good shape, amid some downward guidance revisions. Leverage ticked higher to 4.05x. Leverage is 0.29x higher than 1Q23's record low, although still well below the long-term average of 4.31x, and well below the peak in 1Q21 at 5.92x. EBITDA expanded from a year ago at a pace of +1.2%, with the strongest gains in the Technology, Media, and Healthcare sectors. Metals/Mining, Retail, and Transportation saw the largest declines in EBITDA. Leverage for BB, B, and CCCs is 3.4x, 4.7x, and 6.8x versus the past decades' average of 3.5x, 5.0x, and 7.6x, respectively. Interest coverage ratios decreased -0.23x to 4.66x but are still above the historical average of 4.50x.

The HY default rate ticked down slightly by 17bp to 1.64%, which is down 141bp since year-end 2023. This represents the lowest default rate in over two years, and for context, the 25-year HY default rate is 3.4%. Notably, leveraged loans are seeing a significantly higher default rate, at 4.49%, which is above their long-term average of 3.0%, and a nearly four-year high. This spread of 285bp over HY is the highest since 2000 as the effects of higher-for-longer policies sink in. HY spreads tightened some in the quarter from +340bp to +318bp, and still remain relatively tight, especially if there is a chance of a recession. As yields moved higher to 7.5% in HY, the all-in yield picture is still meaningful, although less generous than the 8-9% earlier in 2023.[4]

Municipal Commentary

Municipal markets followed the US Treasury market, with a substantial selloff over Q4 that began in mid-September, partially retraced in November and resumed in December. The move to higher yields had a negative impact on performance with investment grade municipals returning -1.22% for the quarter, beating US Treasury returns (-3.14%), which in turn outperformed taxable municipal bonds (-3.57%). The Treasury curve shift to higher yields was driven by persistent inflation, concerns both before and after the election about the potential inflationary impact of a new Trump administration's policies, and the FOMC taking a more hawkish stance. The table below shows the change in AAA municipal yields in basis points across the curve during the quarter.[3]

From a relative value perspective, tax-exempt bonds are cheaper than a year ago at all but the 30-year tenor, but continue to be historically rich relative to Treasuries across the curve. AAA Muni/Treasury ratios moved slightly higher at 2 and 5 years and slightly lower at 10 and 30 years over the quarter, rising from 63% to 66% in the 2 year and 63% to 64% in the 5 year, while falling from 66% to 65% in the 10 year and 80% to 75% in the 30 year. Though lower than Q4 2023's high yields, nominal rates in municipal bonds remain close to the highest yields in the last decade. Municipal Fund flows were approximately $40 billion for the year in 2024, with 23 consecutive weeks of inflows in the latter half of the year.

The municipal bond market will continue to take overall direction from US Treasury markets, with relative value being dependent on market technicals. We're seeing projected reinvestment capital of around $400 billion and projected issuance of about $450 billion for 2025. Fund flows will dictate whether or not the projected net supply puts upward pressure on ratios. If the economy remains strong, capital flows will trend to riskier sectors and poor municipal flows could result in rising ratios. Should the economy exhibit signs of weakness, the credit quality of municipal bonds relative to corporates has historically driven strong flows to the municipal market which will support strong relative value and lower ratios.[3,4]

Outlook

When almost all the sell-side forecasts are tightly grouped within a narrowband, complacency abounds. 10-year U.S. Treasury yield forecasts within a sideways to higher range are the product of mild at best progress on inflation, coupled with inflationary pressures from tariff, immigration, and other fiscal policies under the new administration. These are valid concerns. However, when seemingly everyone thinks rates are stuck higher for longer and future government action can only be inflationary, we look for reasons to be contrarian.

Firstly, the recent surge in long-term rates is a problem for many consumers and businesses. Many of them believed that rates were going lower and were optimistic. Perhaps this allowed consumers to continue spending beyond their means in Q3/Q4 (as evidenced by further declines in excess savings, and spending below income levels). The continued strength in retail sales has been led by the wealthy, with cracks forming under the surface, including credit utilization and delinquencies on the rise. Maybe businesses retained employees or added more and made plans to increase capital investment in 2025. The cost of capital for most people and businesses is priced off of longer-term rates, not Fed Funds, and money has gotten a lot more expensive over the last few months. Financial conditions have tightened as a result. At some point, presumably, this would put the brakes on spending.

Secondly, the amount of tariffs and how they will be structured is uncertain. If they are targeted towards certain countries or certain products, the impact would be less than if they were broad-based. There is some debate as to whether tariffs are even inflationary at all, as they provide a 1-time boost to prices, but not a continuing expectation of future price increases, so they are a step higher in prices but not a persistent inflation threat. Also, if the retaliatory response crimps demand for exports, it could also favorably affect the supply / demand balance and ease pricing pressures domestically. Let's see whether tariffs are initiated as negotiating tools, or permanent, and then make a more reasoned assessment.

Thirdly, action on immigration could also have a varied impact, depending on the amount and structure. The incoming administration is cognizant that the US labor force depends on immigrant labor, especially in certain industries, and is unlikely to disrupt corporate workforces on a widespread basis. If immigration is more targeted, it should not lead to widespread wages and price inflation. Also, removing people from the country would also remove aggregate demand for goods and services, in a counterbalancing impact.

All these potential policies could be inflationary, or not. They could foster growth, or a negative shock. The reaction function from individuals and businesses, both here and abroad, could also go in a variety of directions. Enacting aggressive or controversial legislation is always a challenge but driving it through the narrowest majority in Congress in nearly 100 years may prove to be insurmountable. The fiscal hawks may demand spending offsets to any proposed legislation. Rather than runaway profligate fiscal spending, we could get restraint. Perhaps this would calm the bond vigilantes and narrow the term premium treasury investors demand to hold 30-year bonds.

Relative valuation between stocks and bonds is a further consideration. The equity risk premium (defined as the excess return that investing in the stock market provides over a risk-free rate) is unfavorable, and if this relationship normalizes, bonds will regain some of their attractiveness as a source of income and a safe haven from turbulence and volatility, especially if or when there is a correction in equities. If nothing else, we should expect volatility over the coming years.

We are not wholly convinced that any of these points above will definitively occur and are not advocating a 100% contrarian view. However, we do find it informative to understand both sides of these arguments so that we can adjust our positioning accordingly. In the meantime, employing all the tactical tools at our disposal in the form of rate hedges, credit hedges, shorter or longer duration, higher or lower credit quality, and even going to more cash at times will all be available to us as we navigate the road ahead.

We thought that there were cracks forming in the labor market last fall. The unemployment rate rose from 3.4% to 4.3% but has since declined modestly back to 4.2%. Job openings in the JOLTS survey fell to a recent low of 7.37 million in September, but have since risen two months in a row back to 8.1 million. There were just 1.1 postings for each person seeking work, back to pre-pandemic levels, and the quits rate has also normalized to pre-Covid levels, down to 1.6% in November. We may need to wait a few months to get past the distortions from natural disasters, rate cut hopes, and election hopes and dreams to see where companies' employment decisions are headed for a true read on the health of the labor market. Either way, wages should not be a source of future inflation, as the Employment Cost Index has fully normalized and sits at 0.8%, consistent with inflation readings at or below 2%.

The Citi Economic Surprise index peaked in mid-November and declined steadily through the year-end. The Atlanta Fed GDPNow Index sits at 2.73% as of writing, but we will keep an eye on revisions in coming weeks to see if late-quarter readings were slowing, consistent with the decline in the economic surprise index. Gross Domestic Income for 1H 2024 was revised higher a few months ago, from 1.3% to 3.2%, largely on account of faster compensation growth. This would suggest that consumer savings did not dwindle as much as the earlier belief. Combined with the wealth effect from equity markets rebounding to all-time highs, consumers should be confident in their ability to keep spending. Yet, the quits rate has further declined to 1.6%, the lowest since April 2020, and other indicators on how individuals are feeling about the job market and their ability to find a new job if fired, or a higher paying job if they switch, are also in conflict with what should be positive consumer sentiment. Psychology suggests that even if consumers have excess savings, growing portfolios, lower inflation, and now lower interest rates, if they have doubts about the labor market, they will be more cautious.

The FOMC is now in a pause, the duration of which will depend on incoming data and incorporating the impact of future policy.

Recent corporate commentary generally offered upbeat outlooks for the balance of 2024 and into 2025; however, those companies focused on serving lower and even middle-income cohorts have been expressing more concern about the demand outlook given the disproportionate burden that inflation and higher rates have on these types of consumers. Numerous automotive OEMs and parts suppliers have recently cut their outlooks substantially on account of demand weakness and bloated inventories. These companies tend to be bellwethers in terms of their predictive impact on other aspects of the economy.

Given where all-in yields are now, we believe they are generally compensating investors for additional spread widening all the way down to the single-B rating tier. Below single-B, you had better get your credit analysis and downside protection correct, as the lack of trading liquidity in that tier severely punishes mistakes.

We believe the sweet spots for future total returns are threefold: high quality shorter duration BBB and BB corporate bonds; certain lower rated single-B and CCC corporate bonds that we believe are stronger and more resilient than the market and have an identifiable path to credit improvement; and some event-driven investments where the still-wide-open credit markets are available to facilitate the transactions to drive these catalysts. Considering rate uncertainty, higher rated investment grade bonds with duration sensitivity are a bit trickier. The recent backup in rates makes them more attractive again, as all-in yields are higher, and the new issue supply should be a bit more muted. Ordinarily, for a no-landing scenario we would make tactical adjustments to add more credit risk to the portfolio, but with spreads already very tight and CCCs having rallied 15.09% in 2024, we'll have to pick our spots rather than indiscriminately buy the entire cohort of lower rated bonds whose dispersion is abnormally high. There are valid reasons to believe spread widening might stop short of previous recessions, as the index has a better-quality composition (more BBs, fewer CCCs), beginning all-in yields are higher than the onset of a typical recession, and the average dollar price of bonds is much lower and much closer to recovery rates. However, if spreads were to blow out to levels above +800 basis points that are often reached in severe recessions, total returns would likely be quite negative. Navigating these bouts of volatility by adjusting credit quality overall and selecting the right individual securities will be the keys to success, and we are confident in our ability to thrive in such an environment.

As always, we will be cognizant of valuations, while continuing to seek value and compelling risk/reward investments across fixed income products with a focus on the corporate and municipal bond markets.

data.title: Sylvamo Corporation: Optimism In The Company's Performance Despite Free Cash Flow Decline In 2025
data.description:
Shares of paper and pulp producer, Sylvamo Corporation (NYSE:SLVM) are up 20.36% (YoY). SLVM announced its Q4 2024 and FY 2024 results where Q4 revenue was up 0.62%(YoY) at $970 million while FY 2024 revenue rose 1.4% (YoY) to $3.773 billion compared to $3.721 billion in the prior year. I am rating this stock as a hold due to operational concerns that may affect the company’s valuation in 2025. However, the company has made important strides since its spin-off from International Paper (IP) in October 2021 from a shareholder and business perspective, which I will also consider in the report.

Financial and Share Performance

Over the past three years, SLVM has outperformed the S&P 500 by 66.88% while IP has only gained 29.22% (in this period).

In the 12-month trailing (TTM) analysis, SLVM’s dividend yield of 2.41% has outpaced the sector average of 2.08% by 15.88% while the forward yield of 2.63% has exceeded the industry average of 2.10% by 25.61%. SLVM realized $632 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2024 and has recorded free cash flow (FCF) of $811 million since 2021.

However, SLVM reduced its adjusted EBITDA guidance for Q1 2025 to the range of $85 million to $105 million (a midpoint of $95 million) down 39.49% (QoQ) from $157 million realized in Q4 2024. There are operational issues such as the price decrease in paper in Europe and Brazil as well as Latin America’s adverse mix that may disrupt business and reduce SLVM’s earnings in 2025.

Valuation, Risks, and Future Perspective

Sylvamo is trading at a forward price-to-earnings (PE) ratio of 10.11X from a 5-year average of 7.23X and a sector median of 21.56X (TTM). It went public in October 2021 (after the spin-off from IP) opening at $29.55 per share and has gained 137.25% (as of this writing). The company reported an EPS of 1.94 as of Q4 2024 with FY 2025 consensus estimates ranging from 6.65 to 7.45. As of this writing, SLVM was trading at $69.95 meaning that the forward PE ratio in FY 2025 will range from 9.38X to 10.52X (giving a midpoint of 9.95X). I believe SLVM may trade at a high of $78.37 by the end of the year if it achieves the forecast EPS (on the higher end) representing an increase of 12.05% (from its current pricing) and a decline of 20.05% (from its 52-week high of $98.02). This metric shows the stock is undervalued at the moment.

While SLVM lowered its EBITDA guidance for Q1 2025 by 39.49%, QoQ (as outlined in the article), a look at the EV/EBITDA multiple supports my higher forecast for the stock. Firstly, SLVM's forward EV/EBITDA (taking into account the lower EBITDA guidance) stands at 5.78X against the industry average of 7.95X (a difference of -27.37%). Sylvamo's 5-year average for this metric sits at 5.09X, further supporting the fair valuation of the stock (based on its historical performance) despite the lower EBITDA guidance into Q1 2025.

In comparison, rival company Graphic Packaging Holding (GPK), with a market cap of $8.16 billion against SLVM’s $2.82 billion, has a 12-month trailing PE of 12.58X. For FY 2025, it provided an EPS guidance ranging from $2.53 to $2.78 (giving a midpoint of $2.66). With its current price at $27.17 per share, I expect the forward PE (by the end of 2025) to stand at 10.21X. These multiples mean the company will trade at a high of $28.38 representing a decrease of 7.56% (from its 52-week high of $30.70).

I would like to consider another company in the packaging segment, Ball Corporation (BALL) with a market capitalization of $14.59 billion. It gave a forward EPS of 3.52 for the FY 2025 against a forward PE ratio of 15.92X. I expect BALL to trade at $56.04 after attaining this EPS. While this future price represents an increase of 8.6% (from the current price of $51.59) it indicates a decline of 21.42% (from the 52-week high of $71.32).

In the wake of these assumptions, this industry is likely to face tumult as a result of a possible decrease in earnings in 2025. However, these companies may execute a turnaround to raise revenue. For instance, Sylvamo intends to invest $145 million to accelerate and modernize one of its paper machines and acquire a new replacement sheeter in South Carolina by the end of 2026.

SLVM’s cash from continued operations declined 6.94% (YoY) to $469 million in 2024 while capital spending increased 5.24% (YoY) to $221 million from $210 million in the prior year. The company is intent on growing its North American division, since it recorded a 19.40% (YoY) decline in CapEx to $54 million in 2024 from $67 million in 2023. Latin America had the highest CapEx in 2024 at $140 million (accounting for 63.35% of SLVM’s expenditure). Notwithstanding, the United States accounted for 53.78% of SLVM’s net sales in 2024 at $2.029 billion, while Brazil followed with $971 million in 2024.

I believe that by injecting $145 million into North America, SLVM intends to boost sales by more than 50% by FY 2026. There is also the 20-year partnership that will allow SLVM to outsource operations of its Eastover woodyard saving the company $75 million in CapEx (almost 34% of the current annual CapEx) over the next 5 years.

On the flip side, Sylvamo’s cash reserves have also declined 6.82% (YoY) to $205 million in 2024 from $220 million realized in 2023. Free cash flow also fell 15.65% (YoY) to $248 million in 2024 from $294 million in 2023. I will calculate the FCF yield to add color to the company’s valuation relative to GPK and BALL.

Bottom Line

I have rated SLVM as a hold, despite future operational concerns related to product pricing that may affect revenue generation. data.title: Teva Pharmaceutical: A Risky Bet With Potential Upside
data.description:
Teva Pharmaceutical (NYSE:TEVA) has had a rough ride over the past few years. Debt, legal issues and pricing pressure in the generics market have weighed it down, keeping investors wary. But the company isn’t standing still. It’s cutting costs paying down debt and trying to shift its focus to higher margin biosimilars and specialty drugs. On paper that sounds promising. The real question is whether it’s enough to justify buying in now—or if patience is still the smarter play.

The stock looks incredibly cheap at the moment trading at 6.56x forward earnings, while the rest of the healthcare sector sits at 19.25x. At first glance, that kind of discount suggests a major buying opportunity. But a closer look shows why Teva hasn’t seen a meaningful re-rating yet. The company’s debt-to-EBITDA ratio has climbed to 40.5x which signals that even though it’s been paying down debt its earnings haven’t kept up. That’s a big problem especially with refinancing deadlines coming up in 2026. The market isn’t going to reward Teva until it proves it can grow its cash flow and keep its margins from slipping further.

Debt Reduction: A Step in the Right Direction But Still a Problem

Teva has made progress on the debt front bringing its total down from $61.7 billion in 2020 to $44.5 billion today. It also cleared $956 million in 6% senior notes and $685 million in 1.13% senior notes that were due in 2024. That’s a solid effort but it doesn’t change the fact that Teva’s leverage ratio has actually gotten worse because its earnings have declined.

The one advantage Teva has right now is time. There are no major debt maturities until 2026 which means the company doesn’t have to scramble for refinancing just yet. That breathing room could be enough to turn things around—if it can stabilize its earnings before then. If not, those looming debt payments will become a major overhang on the stock.

Profitability and Cash Flow: Margins Are Still Too Tight

Revenue in Teva’s U.S. business grew 4% to $8.03 billion driven largely by AUSTEDO and biosimilars but that growth hasn’t been enough to offset other pressures. Gross margin dropped from 55.7% to 54.6% showing that Teva is struggling to maintain profitability. At the same time selling and marketing expenses jumped by 11.8, a sign that the company is pushing hard to establish itself in new markets but also that it’s spending more to do so.

Teva’s operating cash flow took a hit falling from $1.368 billion in 2023 to $1.247 billion in 2024. Some of that decline comes from tax payments but the core issue remains: profitability isn’t improving fast enough. The company is investing in biosimilars and specialty drugs which have better margins than generics but those investments are still in the early stages. For now, it’s a waiting game to see if they’ll pay off.

Biosimilars: A Necessary Bet But Execution is Key

Teva has been pushing hard into biosimilars and it’s easy to see why. Unlike generics, biosimilars don’t see their prices collapse as quickly which makes them a more sustainable revenue stream. The company recently launched SELARSDI, its biosimilar to Stelara which gives it a foothold in the immunology market. It also has biosimilars for Prolia Simponi and Simponi Aria in the pipeline all awaiting regulatory approval. If these launches go well, they could be a meaningful source of long-term revenue.

One of the bigger moves Teva has made is rolling out SIMLANDI, a biosimilar to Humira which puts it in direct competition with major drugmakers. The Humira market is massive, but competition is fierce. Pricing and reimbursement negotiations will be key and success isn’t guaranteed. The biosimilars strategy makes sense but the real test is whether Teva can gain traction in a crowded field.

Why is the Stock So Cheap?

Teva’s valuation makes it look like a bargain. It’s trading at 6.56x forward P/E while the rest of the healthcare sector averages 19.25x. Its EV/EBITDA sits at 7.11x well below the 12x-14x range where larger pharmaceutical companies trade. On a price-to-sales basis it’s at just 1.12x compared to the sector median of 3.60x.

If Teva were a stable growing company, these numbers would scream undervaluation. But the reality is more complicated. The stock is cheap because of high debt shrinking EBITDA and uncertainty around its biosimilars strategy. Until Teva proves it can execute the market isn’t going to reward it with a higher multiple.

If the company can successfully grow its biosimilars business and expand margins a 10x forward earnings multiple would put the stock at $25-$30. But if profitability remains weak and debt remains a concern it could easily stay stuck below $20 or even drop toward $14 if things go south.

The Risks That Could Derail a Recovery

Even though Teva is moving away from generics that part of the business still makes up more than half of its revenue and pricing pressure isn’t letting up. The industry is becoming more competitive and the company’s gross margins are already showing signs of strain.

Debt is another major issue. Even after paying down billions a 40.5x debt-to-EBITDA ratio is still dangerously high. If interest rates stay elevated or refinancing in 2026 becomes more expensive it could create additional pressure on the balance sheet.

The legal overhang isn’t completely gone either. Most of the opioid-related lawsuits have been settled but Teva is still making settlement payments that drag on cash flow. The company is also under a Deferred Prosecution Agreement with the DOJ which means any new compliance issues could lead to hefty fines or restrictions.

The biosimilars push is promising but it’s not a guaranteed win. Teva has less experience in this market than some of its competitors and convincing doctors and insurers to switch patients to its biosimilars won’t happen overnight. If these products don’t gain traction quickly it could put more pressure on the company’s bottom line.

Is Teva a Buy? A Hold? Or a Stock to Avoid?

Teva isn’t in the free fall it was a few years ago but it’s still in a tough spot. The company needs to show consistent earnings growth before investors will take it seriously again. The stock is cheap but that’s because of real risks—not just because the market hasn’t noticed it yet.

At $16.78 per share, there’s upside if biosimilars start delivering and a price range of $22-$25 is possible if Teva can get its margins up. But there’s also real downside risk especially if earnings don’t improve before 2026 when major debt maturities kick in.

For investors willing to take on risk, Teva might be worth a small speculative bet. But for most it’s a hold until there’s more evidence that the turnaround is working. The company has potential, but it still has a lot to prove.

data.title: Teva Pharmaceuticals: A Risky Bet With Potential Upside
